Freud and the Neurosciences: from Brain Research to the Unconscious. Edited by Gieseler Guttman & Inge Scholz-Strasser. Vienna: Verlag der Österreichischen Academie der Wissenshaften. Pp. 114 £14.90 (paperback).

Sue Davison

Sue Davison

Sue Davison  

This little monograph contains the contributions of a group of distinguished scholars brought together for a conference in Vienna in the Spring of 1997 by the Sigmund Freud Society and the Austrian Academy of Sciences. Their purpose was to review Freud's early anatomical and neurological research in the light of discoveries made possible by modern technical advances in neurophysiology and neuro-psychology.

95

The contributions vary in their accessibility and relevance to a general readership, but two in particular stand out as valuable reviews of their respective topics. Oliver Sachs reminds us of Freud's twenty-year apprenticeship as a neuroscientist before he wrote his visionary Psychology for Neurologists or Project for a Scientific Psychology (1895). In it he attempted to

…bring together the domains of memory, attention, consciousness, perception, wishes, dreams, sexuality, defence, repression, primary and secondary thought processes into a single coherent picture of mind and to ground all of them in a basic physiological framework….

and this was before he embarked on the psychoanalytic project which occupied the remaining thirty-four years of his life!.

Morris Eagle reviews the experimental evidence that validates Repression as a valid psychological construct and coping style which, while it confers the benefit of reduced anxiety levels, exacts a price in increased vulnerability to a wide range of somatic pathology.

These two chapters alone will reward the general reader, while specialists will find much to interest them in the remainder. The seven chapters are separated by facsimile reproductions of Freud's histological drawings.

As a medical student Freud conducted research on the neuro-anatomy of the lamprey, the gonad of the eel and the nervous system of the crayfish in the Physiological Institute headed by Ernst Brücke. Driven by the necessity to earn a living, he specialised in neurology, studying under Charcot in 1886. He wrote a series of well-respected reviews of cerebral palsy in children and a major study of aphasia. Meanwhile he continued his neuro-anatomical studies. He developed a new method of staining nervous tissue for histological examination which allowed him to describe the development of nervous tracts in the Medulla Oblongata. He came very close to proposing a neuronal theory of the nervous system in 1882, but the honours go to Cajal and Waldeyer who reached the same conclusion a decade later.

Jeremy Holmes  

I take as my starting-point the themes of the recently published NHS Framework for Mental Health, which could boost the psychological therapies in a number of ways, particularly by its acknowledgement of the effectiveness of psychotherapy. I go on to draw comparisons between the role of the psychotherapist and that of the GP, showing how, although primary-care practitioners should not seek to turn into psychotherapists, they could usefully rely more on language, narrative and metaphor in their everyday work.

[[RunningHead]]

Belief and Imagination: Explorations in Psychoanalysis. By Ronald Britton. Routledge. Pp xii + 226. £17.99.

Marilyn Lawrence

Marilyn Lawrence

Marilyn Lawrence  

This is one of the latest in the New Library of Psychoanalysis, edited by Elizabeth Spillius. It is a deep and complex book, many-layered and difficult to summarise.

Entitled Belief and Imagination, the book's sub-title I think more aptly encompasses the range of the work. It is at once hugely broad: a treatise on human nature. At the same time, it is a carefully focused book about the Oedipus complex. Ronald Britton locates himself as a British post-Kleinian. It is perhaps this re-emphasis on the centrality of the Oedipus situation in all fields of human endeavour, founded on a more-or-less securely established loving relationship with a primary maternal object, signified by Bion's notion of containment, which marks out this increasingly distinctive school of thought.

Although the product of fifteen years’ work and bringing together a number of previously published papers, the book is a pleasingly integrated work.

For Britton, the ‘epistemophilic impulse’, the wish to know and be known, is not a component of love and hate, but rather a drive in itself, but mingled with and complicated by the other two. The depressive position, as outlined by Klein and the Oedipus situation are, for Britton

..inextricably intertwined in such a way that one cannot be resolved without the other; we resolve the Oedipus complex by working through the depressive position and the depressive position by working through the Oedipus complex.

Throughout the book, Britton examines the different ways in which belief masquerades as knowledge, always acknowledging the difficulty of giving up our beliefs and the work of mourning which is required to do so. But what is imagination? Where, psychically, is it located? This question resonates throughout the chapters. He presents his ideas on the fundamental importance of finding triangular space, the space which is encompassed by the parents’ relationship to each other and to their children. Here we are on the trail of the imagination.

Chapters 1-8 are essentially clinical accounts of our attempts to evade the Oedipus situation and the depressive position. These chapters are a clinical tour de force, and touch on, and often build upon, ideas which we have encountered through the works of Segal, Joseph, Malcolm and Steiner.

[[RunningHead]]

Robert (Bob) Gosling, OBE 1920-2000

Formerly Chairman, Tavistock Clinic, London

Anton Obholzer

Anton Obholzer

Anton Obholzer  

Bob Gosling, who died aged 79, was in his day one of the most influential psychiatrists in the country — being both a psychotherapist and a psychoanalyst to many professional colleagues and their spouses, and an inspiration to generations of colleagues and students at the Tavistock Clinic.

Gosling led the Tavistock Clinic from 1968 to 1979, and during this period crafted the strategic vision that has been implemented since then. He was the first and foremost of the new generation of psychiatric staff who shaped the Clinic, following the retirement of Jock Sutherland as chairman. His style was a much more facilitating and participatory one than Sutherland's had been, and led to a flowering of creativity in the institution. His emphasis was determinedly on valuing the contributions of all members of staff and of all disciplines equally. In this he followed the ideals of the Clinic's founders, in creating a truly multidisciplinary organisation with taproots in a variety of professions, ideals and theories.

Born in Birmingham, Gosling became a medical student there, and obtained a BSc in physiology. He then gained a Rockefeller scholarship to do his clinical training in the US, at Cornell. After obtaining his MD there, and qualifying on his return to the UK, he worked as a locum general practitioner before starting his psychiatric training at the Maudsley Hospital as registrar to Professor Sir Aubrey Lewis.

He then became a senior registrar in the Adult Department of the Tavistock Clinic, acting for several years as assistant to the psychoanalyst Michael Balint, who was pioneering training for general practitioners. Gosling qualified as a member of the British Psycho-Analytical Society in 1958, and became a consultant at the Tavistock Clinic.

Bob was much beloved by his patients and colleagues for his warmth, tolerance and deep understanding of the vicissitudes of the human condition. He himself acknowledged that his own suffering and experience as a patient, of which he had more than his fair share, moulded his attitude to patients, colleagues, and to life in general.

Clinical and Theoretical Observations on Atypical Facial Pain

Ruth Freeman and Philip-John Lamey

Philip-John Lamey

Grosvenor Road, Belfast BT12 6BP.

Philip-John Lamey  

This paper examines patients with atypical facial pain. This condition forms part of the subject of oral medicine with patients being referred for pain relief. For the most part patients are unwilling to entertain the idea that their pain could have a psychological causation and it could be said that they are fixed to the physical nature of their condition. From a series of clinical observations it is proposed that the condition of atypical facial pain is not a homogenous entity despite the fact that the presenting symptom - pain - is common to all patients. To make a differentiation between cases it is necessary to identify the immediate cause and the psychical reactions to it.

responseSet.title: On Making a Documentary Film on Psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Making a Documentary Film on Psychoanalysis

Michael Brearley

Michael Brearley

20 Provost Road, London, NW3 4ST
email: brearley@globalnet.co.uk

Michael Brearley  

Psychoanalysis is an intimate relationship. Two people meet regularly in a quiet room. One lies on the couch. They talk about matters of the greatest importance to at least one of them. Complete privacy is intrinsic to the process. The intimacy is not enacted but monitored, studied and used for understanding.

Privacy means exclusion. Entry is forbidden. Such a scenario inevitably arouses curiosity. What are they up to? I remember my 6-year-old son asking me why I wouldn't change places with him: I could go to his school while he would lie down on my analyst's couch. No one is above a temptation to peep through the keyhole; no one lacks oedipal wishes.

Such a prospect is also, naturally, alluring to film-makers, whose art is a sublimation (as Kieslowski affectionately portrayed in Three Colours Red [1994]), of voyeurism and tapping in on telephone conversations. A similar point is made savagely in the perverse use of a camera in Michael Powell's powerful film, Peeping Tom (1960). Portraying secrets is a sort of second nature to the director. And ever since Ernst Pabst's great film — Secrets of a Soul (1926)-made rather against the wishes of Freud, but with the help and advice of Abraham and Sachs, film directors have attempted, in feature film and on television, to portray the dramas of psychoanalysis. Lady in the Dark (1944) and Spellbound (1945) were two notable early examples.

Secrets of a Soul may well have been based on an actual case of a patient propelled into his symptom — he became unable to use a knife — by his unconscious wish to murder his wife, wishes intensified by his witnessing an actual murder; but the film was not a documentary. And the problems arise in just this area. My question is: could a documentary on psychoanalysis portray actual sessions without infringing damagingly on the process itself? I will suggest that it can't.

And this is frustrating. A well-made and sensitive documentary on psychoanalysis would undoubtedly do credit to a film-maker and benefit psychoanalysis, while both informing and entertaining the public. And it might seem that such a film would be the most direct means of informing the public about our work and ideas at a time when they are in competition with so many other psychotherapeutic methods and methodologies.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy: Past, Present and Future
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy: Past, Present and Future

Antony Garelick

Antony Garelick

Antony Garelick  

In 1895 Freud presented his paper ‘Project for a Scientific Psychology’, an ambitious attempt to construct a neuro-psychological theory of mind. His abandonment of the project and his discovery of psychoanalysis led to major discoveries about unconscious mental processes, psychic determinism, infantile sexuality and remarkable insights into our internal worlds which are a framework for understanding various aspects of human behaviour. These discoveries have profoundly affected our culture and have led to psychoanalytic treatments being available in the NHS in modified form. Freud's’ work was found to be radical and disturbing, resulting in hostility and resistance which is still with us today over a century later. Resistance to the pursuit of knowledge about the self remains ubiquitous.

Over the last century new areas of knowledge have developed leading to an array of new clinical treatments from medication to a range of psychological and psycho therapeutic treatments. Psychoanalytic psychotherapy has to make its case in a crowded and rivalrous therapeutic domain.

The Modernist Philosophy

In the public sector the modernist philosophy has been the key influence. Modernism is a product of the enlightenment espousing emancipation through knowledge, but to some extent has been usurped with increasing emphasis on reason, order and stability. It is based on the belief that logical rules that can be measured lead to rational assumptions that can explain the world (Muir Gray 1999). The government of the day is investing great hope that this will bring a rational/scientific base to the practice of medicine and become the engine for improvement in standards of clinical practice. However, as with most things in life, new ideas or developments, can be used both constructively and unconstructively. A commonly expressed anxiety is that this vehicle could be used as a mechanism for rationing both care and the provision of clinical services.

The recently published National Service Framework (NSF) document resembles in many respects the national curriculum for education. It is a useful model from which we can learn.

[[RunningHead]]

Erotic Transference and Countertransference: Clinical Practice in Psychotherapy. Edited by David Mann. Routledge. Pp. viii + 190.

Cleo Van Velsen

Cleo Van Velsen

Cleo Van Velsen  

Whilst investigating the representation of psychaoanlysis in film, I found that a major theme, from Spellbound to Prince of Titles, involved the analyst and patient coming together as a loving/sexual couple.

104

This stereotype, or caricature, of the patient's falling in love with the analyst, and vice versa, generalises to modern culture as a whole. It also exercised Freud in the early days of psychoanalysis, especially when it directly affected his protégés such as Jung and Ferenczi. This is ironic when, as David Mann and others point out in this volume, a full discussion of the erotic transference and countertransference has been lacking in the psychoanalytic literature of the last fifty years until relatively recently. Mann's aim is not to be

…fully representative of this extensive topic. Rather, it is the intention to illustrate how erotic narratives may be understood in different tongues.

To this end, the ten contributors represent a ‘wide variety of psychoanalytic perspectives’, namely Kleinian, Lacanian, Jungian, Kohutian and Independent. There are three broad sections, with overlap, examining ‘Love and the erotic’ from a developmental point of view, ‘Progressive and regressive aspects of the erotic transference and countertransference’ and ‘Sexual abuse and its impact on psychotherapy’. I recognise, from my own practice, the sentiment expressed by Tauber, and quoted by David Mann:

I have thought for a long time that what strongly blocks us is a profound unease in dealing with love, affection and tenderness in our work; we have acknowledged the need to deal with anxiety, hate, rage etc. but are unclear about and evasive with love, affection and tenderness.

This compact volume goes a long way towards helping one to think about, and conceptualise, the many faces of Eros, ranging from sexual feelings and their enactments, to love as something separate from the sexual. In a climate where boundary violations in therapy have been shown to be more prevalent than previously thought, it behoves all psychoanalytic psychotherapists to tolerate, understand and interpret, when appropriate, not only the erotic aspects of the transference but, perhaps more importantly, the disturbing, exciting and repellent thoughts, feelings and fantasies to be found in the countertransference.

Working Intersubjectively: Contextualism in Psychoanalytic Practice. By Donna M Orange, George E Attwood & Robert D Stolorow. Hillsdale New York: The Analytic Press. Pp 109. £24.50.

Martin Weegman

Martin Weegman

Martin Weegman  

Psychoanalysis, now a hundred years old (and young) stands not so much as one model, but as a matrix from which innumerable models have appeared over the decades, whether or not accompanied by ‘schools’. The ‘intersubjective approach’, whose ideas have accrued over the last twenty years, represents a particularly exciting departure in American psychoanalysis, well away from the not-so-secure base, as the authors would argue, of ‘classical’ analysis. Working Intersubjectively is a short and useful update of some of this research, particularly as it pertains to psychoanalytic practice.

To start with, Working Intersubjectively presents us with an overview of principles — the implications of seeing psychoanalysis as a complex, mutual field involving the subjectivities of both patient and analyst, and of conceiving development (and pathology) as irreducibly relational and embedded in many intersubjective contexts. We are led decisively, it is argued, from the ‘myth of the isolated mind’ and from the concept of development and treatment as the product of, largely, intrapsychic forces. While linked to other approaches — such as Self Psychology (Kohut), relational theory (Mitchell), empirical psychoanalysis (Stern, modern infant research) and the Independent tradition in Britain, to name a few — the intersubjective approach aims to push contemporary thinking forward to new consequences.

Joanne Stubley

London NW3 5BA.

Joanne Stubley  

In 1989, a Californian jury was asked to deliberate on the case of a man accused of the rape and murder of an 8-year-old girl more than twenty years before. The prosecution had as its central witness the accused man's daughter, who alleged she had been present when this crime was committed. However, her memory for the event had apparently emerged for the first time during psychotherapeutic treatment two decades later. The stage was set for a legal battle, with shots fired by the opposing expert witnesses on the reliability of this memory. Whether it was ‘false memory’ or ‘recovered memory’ was the question set to decide the fate of the accused.

The Franklin/Lipsker case is, perhaps, the extreme end of this controversy, when debate becomes war. And yet, even through the smoke of battle, one may begin to glimpse the complex issues this case rests upon. What is truth? Is there a need to distinguish between legal, narrative, historical and scientific truths? How do expert witnesses bring to this legal battle such polarised and conflicting views on the nature of trauma and memory?

Gillian, a university student, consults a clinical psychologist about her problems of low self-esteem, feelings of vulnerability and uncertain sense of identity. In the course of taking a wide-ranging history, the psychologist enquires whether Gillian had suffered any unwanted sexual experiences as a child. Gillian replies ‘No’, but suddenly feels disorientated and unreal. On leaving the consultation she feels confused and has difficulty in thinking. She develops a headache. Later that evening she experiences suddenly an apparent memory of her grandfather sexually abusing her. She feels very anxious and vomits.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Unseen Frame in Professional Cultures

Sylvia O'Neill

Sylvia O'Neill

21 Claremont Crescent, Edinburgh EH7 4HX.

Sylvia O'Neill  

This paper offers a model of healthy and pathological functioning in the professions generally, illustrated with specific discussion of psychoanalytic psychotherapy. Using Bleger's concept of the unseen frame, it is proposed that when a profession is established at local or institutional level, it passes through an initial symbiotic dependence on the frame, conceived of as an aspect of the relationship with the local founder or early leaders. If, however, the necessary development from symbiosis to independent functioning fails, the frame then becomes a stabilised organisation to which the ego of the individual adapts and limits itself in order to go on belonging. Since an incomer to such a culture threatens to expose and shatter the unseen frame, pressure is brought to bear on the incomer to adapt. An incomer who represents core-professional principles may come to pose a threat felt to be extreme, leading to a rapid ‘figure-ground’ type of oscillation between recognition and non-recognition of what is at stake. Under extreme threat, the culture may render the incomer and the associated core professional principles taboo, resulting in further impoverishment of the culture.

The Symmetry of God. By Rodney Bomford. Free Association Books. Pp. 165. £40 hardback, £15.95 paperback.

Margaret Arden

Margaret Arden

Margaret Arden  

What is a review of a theological book doing in a psychotherapy journal? The answer is in the word symmetry, which here means the symmetrical logic of the psychoanalyst Ignacio Matte Blanco. Freud's concept of primary and secondary process are replaced in Matte Blanco's theory by symmetrical and asymmetrical logic. His system of bi-logic describes the way the two processes, imagination and rational thinking, are combined in mental life. The essence of symmetrical thinking is that in the unconscious there is no contradiction, so that any statement implies its opposite. Emotional experiences are free from the constraints of time and causality. Rodney Bomford uses bi-logic to give an account of the meaning of Christianity.

Matte Blanco was a Chilean psychiatrist who came to London to train as a psychoanalyst in the 1930s. He also studied mathematical logic. After spending the War years working as an analyst in the USA, he returned to Chile to take the chair in psychiatry at Santiago. He spent many years studying the speech of schizophrenic patients, publishing his results in the International Journal of Psycho-Analysis in 1940, 1943 and 1959. In 1960 he moved to Rome as a training analyst, and died there in 1995. His most important work, the Unconscious as Infinite Sets was published in England in 1975. His second book, Thinking, Feeling and Being followed in 1988.

Although Matte Blanco had a following in Chile and Rome, he would have been unknown in this country if it had not been for Eric Rayner, who wrote a paper about him in 1981. Rayner realised the potential of Matte Blanco's work for understanding unconscious mental processes. A small group of interested people gathered around Rayner and formed the London Bi-logic Group. Rodney Bomford was invited into the group, not because of his theological credentials but because of his background in mathematics. From the outset, the group was multi-disciplinary.

[[RunningHead]]

Talking Cure: Programme 2

Elizabeth Dodge

Elizabeth Dodge

Elizabeth Dodge  

BBC 2's ‘Talking Cure’1 brought a range of the therapeutic work of the Tavistock Clinic to television. The second programme, featuring family therapy, showed Barbara Dale's and Jenny Altschuler's work with families where a parent has a serious illness. A major aspect of the programme was to show the importance of offering therapy for seriously ill parents (Altschuler & Dale 1999). Families may need the opportunity to address relationship issues with therapists who are not responsible for treatment of the illness itself.

Juliette was such a parent. She was awaiting a heart-lung transplant following a late diagnosis of cystic fibrosis made at the time of the premature birth of her daughter Amy, a few years before. Juliette became critically ill at this time and nearly died herself. Over the course of therapy the audience was shown the way the therapists worked as a team, on this occasion Barbara in the room with the family and Jenny behind the one way screen, taking a consultation break during the session. Working in this way as a team, although very familiar to family therapists, can feel quite alien to others, but brings the opportunity to offer a different perspective in the course of the work.

The first meeting between Juliette and Barbara showed her telling her distressing story as if it were yesterday, giving a sense of being stuck in the pain of the time of the diagnosis, her daughter's premature birth and her own critical illness. Barbara avoided being overwhelmed by the “dazzle factor” of Juliette's distress (Dale and Altschuler 1999) in order that she could be free to draw out the ‘inside story’.

Therapy focused on different aspects of Juliette's relationships that have been affected by the illness. The impact of parental illness on children is a major one and Barbara was able to involve Amy in the sessions, whilst remaining alert to Juliette's wish to protect her. She introduced the idea that Amy was more resilient than her mother may believe. Amy's understanding was brought out again in a later session when Amy's comment that Mummy could not play with her was viewed as a further strength in the family where Amy felt safe enough to voice these thoughts which might remain unspoken in order to protect her mother from distress.

David Barbenel  

The presence of complaints procedures is an essential element of clinical management. We discuss diagnostic, psychodynamic and systemic factors that may lead patients and their carers to make ill-founded complaints against their psychiatrists. We argue that explicit attention to these factors is essential for effective understanding and investigation of complaints in the NHS Mental Health services.

[[RunningHead]]

‘Home is Where the Hate is’: Analytic Perspectives on the Modern Mental Home

Mike Swinburne

Mike Swinburne

28 Staveley Close London N7 9RS.

Mike Swinburne  

This paper attempts to describe some of the unconscious processes at work within the modem community mental home, using Kleinian and post-Kleinian ideas to look at the concept of home itself in relation to the difference in functioning between the psychotic and non-psychotic mind, and observing the implications this has in terms of the task facing community care staff and home managers. The paper relies on the central hypothesis that the minds of the borderline and schizophrenic individuals who tend to make up the bulk of residents within community mental homes function in a manner akin to that of the early infant prior to the development of internal space via the introjected experience of a containing maternal object relationship. It argues that as a consequence of this the challenge facing staff working in such environments becomes one of replicating maternal containment as the prerequisite to therapeutic aspirations or notions of meaningful change for individuals from within their client group.

[[RunningHead]]

Panic Attacks Late in Life

Jane Garner and Jan Abram

Jan Abram

Jan Abram  

From Dr Jane Garner:

Dear Editor

The work which Joan Hunter described in the latest APP journal1, the treatment of an 82-year-old woman for panic attacks, raises two important issues for those working with older adults: the re-emergence of early conflicts and trauma with increasing dependency in old age and the need for a flexible approach to treatment.

With regard to the second point, external reality intrudes into the therapy with older people in the form of physical handicap, immobility and sensory impairment in ways that need to be accommodated. Not to give a hand to a patient struggling to stand could be considered abusive. Ardern (1997) reminds us to recognise the social aspects of a therapy group for patients leading isolated lives constrained by physical and financial problems as much as by psychological factors.

Jung (1931), who thought that psychotherapy could be extended to the second half of life, suggested there should be some modifications in technique. Working with patients who have a diagnosis of dementia requires further modification (Hausman 1992): the therapist may even need to serve some memory function for the patient whose ego is under siege.

Hunter describes the treatment of her patient as unavoidably ‘unorthodox’, as it involved individual and group therapy as well as telephone sessions. For particular groups of patients, therapy is only an option if it is unorthodox. However, that is not to say that the underlying principles are different. The theoretical base is perhaps doubly important so that one is always mindful of when and why one might be departing from it. Hunter is to be congratulated for her work.

Talking Cure: Programme 1

Maureen Marks

Maureen Marks

London SE5 8AB.

Maureen Marks  

I watched the first of the recent BBC series Talking Cure1, which presented the work of The Tavistock Clinic, with curiosity, excitement and anxiety. The Tavistock's decision to allow their work to be filmed and presented on TV is a breakthrough for psychoanalysis and psychotherapy, and represents a major move towards a greater openness to and engagement with the world at large. Some might argue that such openness could lead to a dilution, even destruction, of psychoanalysis and psychoanalytic ideas — like mothers who cannot bear anyone intruding into their relationship with their babies, because they fear this may harm their special relationship or even the child itself. Others will suggest that this move towards a dialogue with the outside world can only be a positive and creative one and gives hope for the future growth of psychoanalysis. My own view is toward the latter, but I have some reservations. One is about the media itself, and how television can so easily trivialise serious encounters, or worse, involve the viewer in something voyeuristically perverse. On balance, the first of the series did not do this. Another important issue concerns the conflict between the need to publicise the work of psychoanalysis and the need to maintain patient confidentiality, even when the patient gives consent. In Talking Cure, patient confidentiality was breached and yet it was this very breach that gave the programme its impact — this was a real therapist doing real work with a real patient.

In his paper on Publication, Ron Britton (1999) discusses the betrayal and guilt which can be felt when we disclose patient sessions in the interests of scientific discourse carried out with colleagues. He suggests that in moving from the subjectivity of the analytic dyad to scientific objectivity, certain oedipal difficulties can be aroused, in particular, those of the father sacrificing his daughter to his continuing alliance with her mother. Britton is not questioning the necessity of publication, but pointing out where some of the difficulties in doing so lie. Kevin Healy

Kevin Healy

Kevin Healy  

This book is one of the Tavistock Clinic series of books which aims to make available the clinical and theoretical work that is most influential at the Tavistock Clinic. The series presents new approaches in the understanding and treatment of psychological disturbance in children, adolescents and adults at an individual and at a family level. It covers issues such as understanding trauma, the growth of the personality, eating disorders, systemic family psychotherapy, psychotic states in children, and the work of Hanna Segal.

In this book, staff of the Adolescent Department examine in accessible language different clinical aspects of adolescent disturbance, exploring in particular its impact on the family.

responseSet.title: Open Minded. Working out the Logic of the Soul. By Jonathan Lear. Cambridge, Mass and London: Harvard Univ. Press. Pp 345. £9.95 pb.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Open Minded. Working out the Logic of the Soul. By Jonathan Lear. Cambridge, Mass and London: Harvard Univ. Press. Pp 345. £9.95 pb.

Robin Cooper

Robin Cooper

Robin Cooper  

‘I want to say that there is something dead in the profession of psychoanalysis and something dead in the profession of philosophy,’ writes Jonathan Lear, in his preface to this imaginative and intelligent book. ‘This book is above all a response to a sense of deadness: it is an attempt to bring some life into two activities which lie at the heart of our humanity.’

What, then, is this deadness? The deadness of philosophy will be all too familiar to those who have approached the subject in search of wisdom, in search of guidance through the perplexities of life, only to have found instead, in philosophy departments where there appears little street-credible evidence of real life, merely endless ‘talk about talk’. If philosophy begins in wonder, perhaps it ends, as Wittgenstein always suggested, when the philosopher's job becomes tenured.

And how is psychoanalysis dead? Whilst it might be more apparently true that psychoanalysis is a practice, a therapy (unlike philosophy, as Bion rather disdainfully states, ‘intended for use’), it displays its own closed-mindedness. Its deadness shows, perhaps as much as anywhere, in its tendency towards arrogance, in the smug complacency of its ‘knowingness’, and its closure with regard to the philosophical tradition. Lear's analysis of Oedipus, the original self-made man, is instructive here.

197

The Practice as Patient: Working as a Psychotherapist in General Practice

Jo O'Reilly

Jo O'Reilly

Jo O'Reilly  

This paper gives an account of working as psychoanalytic psychotherapist in a GPs' surgery from a base within a local Psychotherapy Department. Particular attention is paid to how the GPs regard the therapist's work, as evidenced by their referrals and other contact with the therapist. An evolving relationship between the therapist and the GPs is described, and material from clinical work and meetings with the GPs is used to illustrate this. The idea of ‘Practice as patient’ is introduced as a model whereby the therapist considers how most usefully to engage with the GPs, and how this can enhance the understanding of the clinical task.

responseSet.title: Reverie and Interpretation. By Thomas H Ogden. Karnac. Pp 295. £18.95.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reverie and Interpretation. By Thomas H Ogden. Karnac. Pp 295. £18.95.

Noel Hess

Noel Hess

Noel Hess  

My familiarity with the work of the American analyst Thomas Ogden was mainly from a previous volume, The Primitive Edge of Experience, in which he discusses the psychopathology of schizoid states (and the psychoanalytic literature is surprisingly sparse on this important subject). He also introduces in that and other preceding books, the notion of the ‘autistic-contiguous position’ to explain pre-paranoid-schizoid mental functioning. This is to put the present volume in the context of the development of his work and thought, although this volume is not concerned with that theory (in fact makes only a brief mention of it), though the interest in schizoid states is continued here. What it is concerned with is the detection, analysis, and interpretation of various forms of aliveness and deadness within the analytic interaction.

Ogden is a rather rare bird, being an American analyst clearly influenced in major ways by British Kleinian thought (especially Bion, Segal and Joseph). One of the interesting aspects of reading Ogden's work is seeing how such ideas are received and used in a context away from, and apart from, the somewhat tribal atmosphere which has tended to prevail in this country (even though this has lessened somewhat in recent years). One senses that this absence of tribal affiliations gives the author a greater ability to integrate ideas from across the different schools of thought, and this makes for refreshing reading.

Although six of the eight chapters are papers published previously, between 1995 and 1997, these papers are well integrated into the overall focus of the book, giving it a cohesion unusual in psychoanalytic texts. I will therefore deal with the book as a whole rather than look at individual chapters.

Ogden's primary concern is with the seemingly paradoxical aim of making deadness alive:

The sense of aliveness and deadness of the transference—countertransference is for me, perhaps the single most important measure of the moment to moment status of the analytic process.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

From Lesley Pavincich

Lesley Pavincich

Lesley Pavincich

Lesley Pavincich  

Dear Editor

I thought that Cleo Van Velsen's comments on Michael Sinason's paper, in her review [last issue of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy, vol. 14, No. 2, 189-191] of the book Psychosis (Madness], misrepresent his views and the analytic work reported in the paper. This chapter, entitled ‘How can you keep your hair on?’, reports on analytic work with a patient who had a compulsion to violently pull her hair out, but who afterwards recovered her ‘usual self’ and was then horrified by the damage done in theses attacks. Van Velsen claims that Sinason's concept of ‘Internal Cohabitation’ will be ‘allowing someone to evade ownership of “madness” by locating it in the “mad” self that can be seen as foreign.’ Sinason describes the analytic work that led the patient to be able to mitigate these attacks on her body and protect herself against them. This was achieved by analytic exploration of ‘who was doing what to whom’ in the internal world. Knowledge of this enabled the patient to gradually be emancipated from the tyranny of the abuse. Sinason's chapter illustrates how responsibility for madness requires detailed analysis of the workings of the internal world and is not achieved by premature demands for taking ‘ownership’ of madness.

279

The Child is Still Ill — How are the Parents?

Abraham Brafman

Abraham Brafman

20 Hollycroft Avenue, London, NW3 7QL

Abraham Brafman  

This paper is based on the Winnicott Lecture given to paediatricians at their Annual Conference in 1999. The theme and arguments put forward are familiar to those engaged in liaison work. The close emotional links and mutual influences between child and parents are stressed, with particular emphasis on the child who develops physical symptoms and is therefore brought to the attention of physicians. Because many of these children fail to improve with the prescriptions of general practitioners and paediatricians, the need to investigate each parent's emotional investment in the situation is underlined. Throughout the paper, Winnicott's theories and his special interest in the child's perspective are repeatedly mentioned.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy in Institutional Settings. Edited by Julia Pestalozzi, Serge Frische, R D Hinshelwood & Didier Houzel. Karnac. Pp 222. £18.95.

Kevin Healy

Kevin Healy

Kevin Healy  

This book links together eleven articles written by authors from eight different countries, mostly within the European Union. All are working within institutional settings set up to treat individuals with severe and enduring mental health difficulties. I begin with some general comments about the book and then proceed to elaborate some common themes running through each chapter. Most of the authors are not writing in their first language. This leads to a rather forced style at times, and a language which struggles to contain and symbolise the concepts being explored. At other times, the language is flowing and poetic but is suddenly jolted by harsh, cruel realities. Initially, I felt ready to criticise the book on these grounds. However, the more I have thought about it the more I realise that such criticisms or comments apply to my own work at the Cassel Hospital. Here too we struggle to contain the pain, distress and fragile emotions of our patients. Finding a common enough language, that facilitates communication rather than thoughtless action, is part of the everyday work here.

It is not easy to condense the thoughtfulness of this book into the space of a book review. There are, however, a number of common themes. First, patients recreate their difficulties within the relationships they form. Each institution must struggle to tolerate complex transferences. Within an institution, transference is often divided, split, refracted and/or defracted in a way that makes living through it tolerable. Individuals (staff and other patients) may serve within rôle and/or within their own personalities as attracters or collectors of particular forms of transference. The everydayness of life in institutions is a screen for projections. It is important to be able to tolerate the countertransference feelings evoked. Patients often enact their difficulties with a level of intrusion and force that may lead to dangerous counter-aggressive measures from staff. Institutional responses need to be continually monitored and considered. Tolerating the transference and countertransference (projective identification is a term not much used in this book) is a major struggle but, if successful, can lead to the containing of severe disturbance and a reliving of the unbearable on the part of patients and staff.

responseSet.title: On Bearing Unbearable States of Mind. By Ruth Riesenberg Malcolm. London: Routledge. Pp. 202. £17.99.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Bearing Unbearable States of Mind. By Ruth Riesenberg Malcolm. London: Routledge. Pp. 202. £17.99.

Erika Bard

Erika Bard

Erika Bard  

This book is a collection of clinical papers given by Riesenberg Malcolm, mostly at psychoanalytic conferences and symposia over the period 1970 to l998. Some of these papers have not been published before and some have been updated. They are a remarkable series of papers, noteworthy for their clarity on what many analysts are concerned with today: the complex issue of the positions of mutual influence in the consulting-room — the ways in which the patient through projective identification exerts a subtle pressure on the analyst to recreate early object relations and the manner in which the analyst as a recipient of these projections is inevitably drawn into this in some way, and under these pressures is ‘recruited’ to enact something of the patient's disturbing primitive object relations. She elucidates these aspects of the analytic situation with alive and authentic clinical material, showing how she, as analyst, had to struggle to contain the projections and to experience the emotional disturbance while sustaining, or indeed recovering, a capacity for reflective thought by working through the countertransference and transference experience. She illustrates this vividly, in eleven papers, where she shows a deep sensitivity to her patients’ often-unbearable projections, and an enormous capacity to find the words which really capture the uncertainties, depth and complexity of the archaic enactments in the transference. This she does on the basis of a working model in her mind based on the theories of Klein, Bion, particularly his theory of reversible perspective, Rosenfeld, Joseph, and her concept of the total transference, Steiner, and his concept of psychic retreats, as well as other contemporary Kleinians.

The book is divided into three sections grouped by subject. There is an excellent introduction to each by Priscilla Roth. The first section is devoted to ‘the internal world in the transference’, and focuses on splitting and projection. Riesenberg Malcolm shows how she follows closely the movements of the internal objects in the transference, and how complex this process of noting these subtle shifts is, especially when the patient splits so much that the analyst may be drawn into following one split and neglecting the other to some extent. She demonstrates to the reader how she describes the countertransference experience to herself to start with, and how part of the work is to find out where and how the contact with the analyst breaks down.

In Defence of NHS Psychotherapy

Phil Richardson and Peter Hobson

Peter Hobson

correspondence: The Tavistock Clinic, 120 Belsize Lane, London NW3 5BA.

Tavistock & Portman NHS Trust and University College London.

Peter Hobson  

This paper takes the form of an imagined conversation between a sceptical interviewer and a psychotherapist from the Tavistock Clinic. The focus of the conversation is whether it is justifiable to practise psychodynamic and systemic psychotherapy in the NHS. Our aim is to explore some (just some) of the options one has in responding to a cross-examination of this kind. We limit the factual content (and, in particular, the research evidence cited) so that the reader may feel armed but not overburdened.

Only Connect: Developmental Issues from Early to Late Life11

Margot Waddell

Margot Waddell

Adolescent Dept. Tavistock Clinic, 120 Belsize Lane, London NW3 5BA.

Margot Waddell  

This paper links aspects of early experience and developmental processes (especially the Oedipus Complex) to troubled states of mind, both in later life and, at length, in the mental predicament of the very elderly — especially in relation to confusion and dementia. The paper draws on psychoanalytic and observational literature, and also on infantile developmental research to trace the ways in which, capacities more familiar in the care of infants and young children are especially relevant in extreme old age — eg an understanding of containment, reverie, projective identification, splitting and denial, etc. through a series of detailed observations from within a single family, the paper explores the ways in which psychoanalytic knowledge of early ‘childishness’ may contribute very immediately, even technically, both to an understanding of ‘second childishness’ (to draw on As You Like It) and to how to work therapeutically with these enfeebled states of mind.

Assessing the Risk of Self-Harm in Adolescents: A Psychoanalytical Perspective11

Robin Anderson

Robin Anderson

120 Belsize Lane, London NW3 5BA

Robin Anderson  

This paper explores the way psychodynamic factors can be used to extend and improve the quality of the assessment of adolescents at risk of self-harm. Conventional risk factors are clearly of value to identify those young people who require admission or other interventions. However, a consideration of the internal and external factors and their interaction, as manifest in the transference and in a consideration of the history and present circumstances, allows shape and depth to be added to the assessment. In this way an assessment can be a therapeutic intervention in its own right, and can play a part in reducing the risk. This approach is described and illustrated with clinical examples.

responseSet.title: Dr John Hunter Padel 1913-1999
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Dr John Hunter Padel 1913-1999

Jane Temperley

Jane Temperley

Jane Temperley  

John Padel was a man of many accomplishments: a classicist, a musician (he played the cello), a doctor, a scholar, a psychoanalyst, but preeminently he will be remembered as a great and gifted teacher of psychoanalysis.

He was born in Carlisle, where his father was headmaster of the grammar-school. He gained a scholarship to Queen's College Oxford, where he read Classical moderations and Greats. He then taught classics — like his father, making a particular contribution to grammar-school education. In 1950 he began retraining as a doctor at the Middlesex Hospital and went on to specialise in psychiatry and to train as a psychoanalyst.

From 1969 to 1977, he was Deputy Director of the London Clinic of Psycho-Analysis. From 1973 to 1979 he was a Senior Lecturer at the Institute of Psychiatry at the Maudsley, where he taught clinical psychotherapy, from 1966 to 1990 he taught on the training programme at the Institute of Psycho-Analysis — on Clinical Psychopathology, on Object Relations and Psychic Reality, on Winnicott, and on Early Applications of Psychoanalysis.

The generosity with which he contributed to clinical work and teaching activities of the Institute of Psycho-Analysis (which are unpaid) was characteristic of him. He was exceptionally facilitating to those at the start of their careers as psychotherapists or analysts, making referrals to them, and even on occasion sharing his own consulting-room.

It was as a teacher of psychoanalytic theory at the Tavistock Clinic that I first remember him, and it is in that context that his ability to communicate learning and an enthusiasm for his subject will have had the greatest effect on mental health professionals. He taught his subject with lucidity and verve, but he also related with great interest and respect to those he taught, enabling them in turn to develop as teachers and practitioners. Like any true teacher, he enjoyed learning — when the Adult Department at the Tavistock had pioneered joint marital work he turned to a junior colleague (a recent student of his) who worked in that unit, and suggested they pair up for what was for him a new approach.

responseSet.title: The Klein—Lacan Dialogues. Edited by Bernard Burgoyne & Mary Sullivan. London: Rebus.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Klein—Lacan Dialogues. Edited by Bernard Burgoyne & Mary Sullivan. London: Rebus.

Ross Skelton

Ross Skelton

Ross Skelton  

The title of this book is The Klein-Lacan Dialogues, and it is tempting to say that there is not much ‘dialogue’ in it — but this is not strictly true. The interchange in ‘Discussion of Papers on Sexuality’ shows the Kleinian Jane Temperley engage thoughtfully with Lacanian ideas. However, in ‘Discussion on the Unconscious’ the non-Lacanians seem completely floored by the notion of the ‘signifier’, which is quite a simple notion. In the ‘Discussion on Child Psychoanalysis’ Bice Benveneuto makes the point that, for Klein, insofar as the father's penis remains in the ‘empire of the mother's body’ it is a persecutor of the paranoid-schizoid position. The father, for Lacan, is not inside mother — he has to be symbolised as something that makes order in the world. This discussion degenerates somewhat over the question of ‘short sessions’ which are, I think, an integral part of the Lacanian approach. Few Lacanians actually vary the lengths of their session dramatically — most seem to favour a more-or-less fixed time, say twenty or twenty-five minutes, and analysts sometimes see the same patient twice in one day. (Must sleep intervene between sessions?) The Lacanian theory is articulated by Geerardyn in ‘The unconscious from a Lacanian point of view’ and Nobus in ‘Theorising the comedy of the sexes’. These essays could not, however, serve as an introduction to Lacan as they are quite sophisticated.

responseSet.title: Making the Case for Dedicated Infant Mental Health Services
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Making the Case for Dedicated Infant Mental Health Services

Paul Barrows

Paul Barrows

Knowle Clinic, Broadfield Road, Bristol, BS4 2UH
e-mail: knowle.clinic@btinternet.com

Paul Barrows  

This paper seeks to demonstrate the case for establishing dedicated Infant Mental Health Services. It is argued that the 0-3 age-group is not well served by the current arrangement for the provision of mental health services along child/adult lines, and falls between these two stools. It is further argued that priority should be given to services for this group because of the immense potential of early intervention programmes. The nature and ethos of such services are discussed, and it is suggested that the time is now ripe for Infant Mental Health to be given institutional recognition as a distinct specialism in its own right.

responseSet.title: Evidence-Based Medicine, Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Evidence-Based Medicine, Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy

Aiden Corvin and Michael Fitzgerald MD FRCPsych

Michael Fitzgerald

Dublin 8

Dublin 2

Michael Fitzgerald  

The scientific status of psychoanalysis is a matter of importance to both theorists and clinicians in psychiatry. The empirical evidence for both the theory and the practice of psychodynamic practice is much greater than is generally recognised. There is strong evidence for the effectiveness of psychoanalysis and psychoanalytically oriented therapy, the future of psychoanalysis depends on this evidence. We will discuss both the evidence and reasons why it is overlooked in certain quarters, and review the developing neuro-cognitive understanding of the mechanisms that underlie psychoanalytical processes.

responseSet.title: The Mystery of Things. By Christopher Bollas. London & New York: Routledge. Pp. 203.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Mystery of Things. By Christopher Bollas. London & New York: Routledge. Pp. 203.

Jessica Yakely

Jessica Yakely

Jessica Yakely  

The Mystery of Things, Christopher Bollas's latest book, is an ambitious and beautifully written treatise that takes us to the heart of the psychoanalytic endeavour. Bollas re-examines the fundamental concepts inherent in the analytic method by looking at the mysterious encounter between the two participants, analyst and analysand. He explores the illusory and deeply evocative nature of the analytic space and therapeutic alliance, where unconscious subjective communication forms new objective psychic life, where both verbal and visual orders operate, where the imaginary and the symbolic can be explored, and where time and space can be apprehended in new ways. By examining the process of psychoanalysis, Bollas exposes forms of knowledge marginalised by Western discourse.

The first third of the book explores the free-associative process, a method that has perhaps been taken for granted among psychoanalysts today, but that Bollas considers central to the analytic enterprise. Bollas considers Freud's discovery of free-association as the most sophisticated method that has been developed in Western culture for unconscious life to matriculate into consciousness, a discovery which has had a revolutionary influence on the history of Western epistemology. The core of Freud's technique was the unconscious communication elicited between the patient's free-association and the analyst's evenly-suspended attentiveness. Free-association runs counter to the logical, rational and conscious method of acquiring ‘scientific’ knowledge promoted by the empiricist tradition.

Bollas argues that other, no less important, forms of knowledge are revealed by the psychoanalytic method which are essential to the treatment of pathological states of mind and to the discovery of psychic truths. Free-association is a paradoxical process, one that by its very destructive nature, through the dissemination of thought, creates new orders of knowing. The dream, Freud's cornerstone of the psychoanalytic movement, is a form of knowledge that is represented by many different unconscious interests condensed into the dream's manifest imagery. Free-association allows the analysand to break up the manifest text of the dream (or the memories, thoughts and feelings evoked and the analytic session) releasing new lines of meaning by creating new stories, memories or texts that in turn break the pathological disturbances of thought and character, or the paralysing effect of the person's symptomatology. Another type of knowing is gained through the analyst's interpretation, which brings together several themes latent in the network of association.
